one_line_takeaway: PROCO uses an LLM-derived conservative cost function plus model-based rollouts to synthesize counterfactual unsafe samples, letting offline safe-RL algorithms detect 'safe-but-infeasible' states and cut constraint violations by 400-1000% on safe-only Safety-Gymnasium datasets, at the cost of substantially lower average reward.
key_finding: On Table 1 (normalized reward r, up-is-better; normalized cost c, down-is-better, safe defined as c<=1), averaged over the 17 tasks: FISOR PROCO achieves r=0.14, c=0.60 versus baseline FISOR r=0.50, c=10.70 -- a claimed 'Safety Improvement' of 1011%. LSPC PROCO achieves r=0.19, c=1.44 versus baseline LSPC r=0.38, c=6.99 (555% safety improvement). CAPS PROCO achieves r=0.11, c=0.69 versus baseline CAPS r=0.38, c=4.96 (427% safety improvement). By the paper's own 'Safe Percent' metric (fraction of 17 tasks with normalized cost <=1): FISOR PROCO is safe on 15/17 tasks, CAPS PROCO on 12/17, LSPC PROCO on 8/17, versus 0/17 for FISOR, 1/17 for CAPS, 2/17 for LSPC, and low counts for the BC-style baselines (BC 0/17, CDT 1/17, CCAC 3/17). The paper adopts FISOR+PROCO as its default reported variant, simply calling it 'PROCO' in later sections. A separate Ant Circle case study (Figure 2) visually shows PROCO's feasible-value estimates correctly flag near-boundary states as infeasible where vanilla FISOR cannot, and PROCO reaches zero safety violations comparable to a 'FISOR Oracle' trained with abundant unsafe data. A feasibility-identification accuracy analysis (Figure 4b) reports that PROCO's classifications closely match the FISOR Oracle's, with near-zero 'Type 2' errors (PROCO calling a truly-unsafe state safe). An LLM-choice ablation (Figure 3d) finds GPT o4-mini and Gemini 2.5 Pro give comparable results while Deepseek R1 yields lower safety performance. A robustness study (Section 5.5, Figure 5) varying the fraction of retained unsafe data (10/20/50/100%) shows PROCO's safety advantage over FISOR holds consistently at low unsafe-data fractions, and only degenerates into a genuine reward-safety trade-off once 100% of the original unsafe data is restored.
core_contribution: The paper proposes PROCO (Model-Based Proactive Cost Generation), an offline safe reinforcement learning framework for settings where the offline dataset contains few or no unsafe (constraint-violating) transitions. PROCO (1) trains an ensemble dynamics model from the offline data, (2) prompts an LLM with natural-language task/constraint descriptions to synthesize a conservative cost function, validated and iteratively refined against a small held-out unsafe set (<=100 transitions) and the safe dataset until it meets an accuracy/conservativeness criterion, and (3) uses the learned dynamics model plus this cost function to perform branched model rollouts that synthesize counterfactual unsafe trajectories. These synthetic unsafe samples are fed into a conservative Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) feasible Bellman operator to identify 'safe-but-infeasible' states (states currently satisfying constraints but that inevitably lead to violation), which then guides feasibility-aware policy learning. PROCO is designed as a plug-in that wraps existing offline safe RL algorithms (FISOR, LSPC, CAPS) rather than a standalone policy learner.
threat_model: N/A - not a security paper. This is a safe-RL / constrained-control paper (avoiding physical constraint violations such as robot collisions or crossing task boundaries) with no adversary, attacker, or adversarial threat model of any kind.
limitations: The dramatic safety gains come with a substantial average reward drop relative to the baselines it wraps -- e.g. FISOR PROCO's average reward (0.14) is roughly a quarter of vanilla FISOR's (0.50), and similarly for LSPC and CAPS variants -- so the paper's own numbers show PROCO trades reward for safety rather than dominating on both axes. The theoretical guarantees (Theorems 4.4, 4.7, 4.8) rest on assumptions the authors acknowledge are idealized: an ensemble dynamics model that is 'calibrated' (Assumption 4.3) or has bounded rollout KL-divergence (Assumption 4.5), a bounded-horizon-to-violation assumption (4.2), and a Lipschitz-style link between state-distribution shift and constraint-violation-function shift (4.6) -- none of which are directly verified empirically beyond downstream task performance. Ablation magnitudes (W/o Model, W/o Relabel, W/o Consv., W/o Refl., etc.) are presented only as a bar chart (Figure 4a) with no numeric values reported in text, limiting precise quantitative claims about each component's individual contribution. The LLM-generated cost function's reliability depends on having at least a small labeled unsafe set (up to 100 transitions/task) for validation/feedback; the paper does not test the fully zero-unsafe-sample regime for this validation step. Results depend on the quality of the learned dynamics model, and the paper itself notes navigation tasks (harder to model) show safety degradation with longer rollout horizons H, whereas velocity tasks (easier to model) do not -- indicating sensitivity to dynamics-model fidelity that is domain-specific. All experiments are in simulated Safety-Gymnasium environments; no real-world or physical robot deployment is reported.

datasets_used: Safety-Gymnasium (17 tasks: 12 navigation tasks = Point/Car robots x Button/Goal/Push scenarios x 2 variants each; 5 velocity tasks = Ant, HalfCheetah, Hopper, Swimmer, Walker2d)
Modified OSRL offline safe RL dataset with all unsafe transitions removed except 100 retained unsafe samples per task for LLM cost-function validation
quantitative_results: Average normalized reward/cost: BC r=0.36 c=4.44; CDT r=0.57 c=7.84; CCAC r=0.08 c=12.55; LSPC r=0.38 c=6.99; CAPS r=0.38 c=4.96; FISOR r=0.50 c=10.70; LSPC+PROCO r=0.19 c=1.44; CAPS+PROCO r=0.11 c=0.69; FISOR+PROCO r=0.14 c=0.60
Safety improvement over corresponding baseline: LSPC+PROCO 555%, CAPS+PROCO 427%, FISOR+PROCO 1011%
Safe task count out of 17 (normalized cost <=1): BC 0/17, CDT 1/17, CCAC 3/17, LSPC 2/17, CAPS 1/17, FISOR 0/17, LSPC+PROCO 8/17, CAPS+PROCO 12/17, FISOR+PROCO 15/17
FISOR+PROCO adopted as the paper's default reported 'PROCO' variant due to best overall performance
baselines_compared: CAPS / CAPS(IQL) (Constraint-Adaptive Policy Switching, soft-constraint extended to hard-constraint setting)
FISOR Oracle (FISOR trained with access to abundant additional unsafe samples, used as a reference ceiling, not a competing baseline)
